authoritative_sources.title: Clothes Captioning: Complying with the Care Labeling Rule
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: The FTC is the primary U.S. regulator for apparel care-labeling requirements. This guidance is the official public operating reference for what care instructions must exist, when warnings are needed, and how care labels must remain attached and legible.
used_for: completion blockers when label records are missing

authoritative_sources.title: Apparel and Labeling
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: FTC apparel-labeling guidance is the official public baseline for textile, wool, and leather-content marketing and labeling requirements that affect apparel and footwear item setup, ticketing, and customer-facing product data.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Item-master, ticketing, and customer-facing product-data validation
used_for: routing of formal labeling or claim interpretation requests

authoritative_sources.title: Marking of Country of Origin on U.S. Imports
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: CBP is the authoritative U.S. customs agency for origin-marking requirements. Apparel operations often depend on country-of-origin records for imported goods, reticketing, and fulfillment handling, but the lane must escalate formal customs conclusions.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Imported-goods intake and origin-marking dependency review

authoritative_sources.title: Guide to U.S. Apparel and Household Textiles Compliance Requirements
authoritative_sources.publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NIST's interagency apparel and household-textiles guide is a recognized public workflow reference because it consolidates the U.S. federal and state record, labeling, and compliance touchpoints that materially affect apparel operations evidence handling.
used_for: named record taxonomy across FTC, CBP, CPSC, and state regimes

authoritative_sources.title: Apparel and General Merchandise
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: GS1 US is the recognized standards body for apparel and general-merchandise identification, product data, and RFID practices used across brand, wholesale, ecommerce, store, and distribution operations.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Style-color-size identity, scanability, and RFID-aware execution
used_for: industry-specific records that distinguish apparel operations from generic retail work

authoritative_sources.title: NIST SP 1181 - Unit Pricing Guide - A Best Practice Approach to Unit Pricing (2025 Ed.)
authoritative_sources.publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NIST's retail-pricing guidance is a recognized public workflow reference for price-display and comparison controls and is relevant when apparel operations work crosses markdown tickets, shelf signs, and ecommerce price presentation.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Pricing, markdown, and price-display exception handling

authoritative_sources.title: GS1 General Specifications
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: The GS1 General Specifications are the core public control standard for GTIN, barcode, and identifier use across apparel item setup, ticketing, receiving, and store or ecommerce execution.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Item identity, ticketing, and receiving evidence control

authoritative_sources.title: GS1 Global Data Model
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: The GS1 Global Data Model is the recognized public standard for the product attributes required to list, store, move, and sell products and is directly relevant to apparel size, color, material, and ecommerce-attribute evidence discipline.

authoritative_sources.title: GTIN Management Standard
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: The GTIN Management Standard is the public control baseline for deciding when a sellable item requires a new trade-item identity, which materially affects apparel style-color-size, pack, and ticketing workflows.

authoritative_sources.title: D5489 Standard Guide for Care Symbols for Care Instructions on Textile Products
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: ASTM International is a recognized standards body and D5489 is the public care-symbol reference harmonized with FTC care-labeling requirements, making it directly relevant when apparel operations handle symbol-based labels and reticketing evidence.
used_for: refusal to approve unsupported symbol-only substitutions

workflow_stages.name: Apparel context and intake grounding
workflow_stages.description: Confirm brand, channel, jurisdiction, style-color-size scope, season or floor-set context, current lifecycle state, and controlling systems of record before accepting the work.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane

workflow_stages.name: Item-master, labeling, and ticketing prerequisite validation
workflow_stages.description: Verify care, content, origin, and ticketing records, style-color-size identity, GTIN posture, price-book version, and launch or floor-set applicability before execution progresses.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane

workflow_stages.name: Order, allocation, and inventory reconciliation
workflow_stages.description: Review OMS, WMS, POS, carton, receiving, transfer, and RFID records to determine whether the requested order, store move, or fulfillment state can safely advance or requires exception handling.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane

workflow_stages.name: Pricing, markdown, and floor-set boundary handling
workflow_stages.description: Preserve price-ticket, markdown-calendar, and floor-set controls and stop when the request would require pricing strategy or financial approval authority.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane

workflow_stages.name: Return, relabel, and reverse-logistics exception progression
workflow_stages.description: Move worn, damaged, relabel, reticket, return-to-stock, or resale-sensitive issues into blocked or rework states with explicit evidence and next-owner routing rather than silent closure.
source_classes: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane

workflow_stages.name: Completion evidence and channel-closeout control
workflow_stages.description: Only close work when system-of-record status, inventory or return evidence, and any stricter tenant controls align across the affected channel, store, or distribution scope.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane

artifacts: style-color-size matrix, item master, GTIN or UPC assignments, season codes, floor-set calendars, and assortment references
care-label, fiber-content, leather-content, country-of-origin, hangtag, packaging, and ticketing approval records as applicable
price books, markdown calendars, promo windows, price-ticket instructions, and approved exception records
purchase orders, ASN and EDI events, carton manifests, packing lists, transfer orders, receipts, and fulfillment confirmations
RFID counts, cycle-count results, WMS and store inventory, OMS events, POS status, and channel-publication records
return authorizations, reason codes, damage inspection notes, quarantine or resale status, vendor chargeback references, and closure confirmations

allowed: Classify apparel execution requests as in scope, out of scope, or blocked on missing prerequisites.
Request the minimum tenant facts, authoritative records, and source context needed to continue safely.
Advance, hold, rework, or complete work based on documented execution state and evidence.
Prepare execution updates, completion records, workflow summaries, and escalation packages without overstating authority.
escalate: Required apparel, label, pricing, order, inventory, return, or tenant-policy records are missing or materially conflict.
The request requires legal interpretation, pricing approval, supplier or material approval, technical-design signoff, customs review, or regulated-product authority.
FTC, CBP, NIST, ASTM, GS1, marketplace, or tenant policy sources materially conflict on label handling, origin, price presentation, completion criteria, or ownership.
A relabel, reticket, markdown, floor-set, return-to-stock, or imported-goods issue changes whether execution may proceed.
refuse: Mark work complete without channel-level or system-level completion evidence.
Approve unsupported label, material, supplier, or pricing changes without documented authority.
Treat the apparel industry label as cosmetic and answer with generic retail operations language that ignores style-color-size, labeling, ticketing, RFID, or return-disposition constraints.

domain_failure_modes: a garment or footwear item is activated with the wrong style-color-size, GTIN, or ticket because item-master and ticketing records were not reconciled
care, material-content, or origin records are treated as optional operational details and a relabel or reticket request proceeds without the controlling evidence
OMS, WMS, POS, carton, and RFID records conflict on inventory truth but the work is still marked complete
markdown or price-ticket changes are executed as if they were routine operations even though pricing authority and calendar approval are missing
returned apparel is moved back to sellable inventory without inspection, condition, or return-disposition evidence
imported-goods origin-marking or labeling questions are answered as operational convenience decisions instead of being escalated to customs or compliance owners
the lane drifts into supplier approval, technical design, pricing strategy, legal interpretation, or product-compliance authority

scenario_family_coverage.scenario_family: Boundary case that would overgeneralize from the industry name
Request that requires a more specialized adjacent lane

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: Footwear-specific public labeling posture varies by material mix and imported-goods context.
unresolved_ambiguities.detail: The public baseline covers care, textile, wool, leather-content, and origin-marking guidance, but tenants still need product-class rules for footwear categories and marketplace-specific attribute requirements before the lane can close all footwear cases with maximum specificity.

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: State law-label and packaging requirements vary for filled or upholstered apparel-related products.
unresolved_ambiguities.detail: NIST's apparel compliance guide identifies state variation in law-label and packaging rules. The lane must rely on tenant retrieval for the applicable state and product scope rather than assuming a single nationwide closure rule.

notes: Authoritative apparel operations records are the style-color-size item master, care and content label approvals, origin records, price books, order and inventory systems, RFID counts, return-disposition artifacts, and completion confirmations designated by the tenant and constrained by FTC, CBP, NIST, GS1, and ASTM baselines.

The lane stops before pricing approval, supplier or material approval, customs conclusions, technical-design signoff, and formal compliance interpretation even when a launch, floor-set, or return deadline is under pressure.
